{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:

In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}

* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}

''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}

=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years=Until [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Higashiyamanashi District]] / [[Nishiyamanashi District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}} [[Category:Defunct districts of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kai Province districts]] [[Category:Former Higashiyamanashi District]] [[Category:Former Nishiyamanashi District]] [[Category:qq]][[category:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamanashi District}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamanashi District}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamanashi District}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yamanashi District]][[category:Yamanashi District]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamanashi District}} [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamanashi District}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamanashi District}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]][[category:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[category:Yamanashi District]][[Category:qq]]

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
