The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyo Ruijusho|ja|和名類聚抄}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|yamanashi trees|ja|ヤマナシ}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Provincial Temple|ja|甲斐国分寺}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}: {{Engishiki shrines list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District|ja|東山梨郡}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District|ja|西山梨郡}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ''' Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家}}. ''' Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|ja|消滅した郡の一覧}}

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|令制国一覧|東山道|甲斐国}} * {{Pathnav|日本|中部地方|山梨県}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|山梨県山梨郡の範囲]] '''山梨郡'''（やまなしぐん）は、[[山梨県]]（[[甲斐国]]）にあった[[郡]]。
山梨県の県名はこの山梨郡に由来している。
== 郡域 == 消滅時の郡域は下記の区域にあたるが、行政区画として画定されたものではない。
* [[山梨市]]の全域 * [[甲州市]]の大部分（勝沼町上岩崎・勝沼町下岩崎・勝沼町藤井・大和町日影・大和町田野・大和町木賊を除く） * [[甲府市]]の一部（黒平町を除く概ね[[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]以東） * [[笛吹市]]の一部（春日居町小松・春日居町国府・春日居町鎮目・春日居町山崎・春日居町松本以北）
[[古代]]の郡域には、これらのほか、笛吹市一宮町各町・御坂町各町・石和町各町を含んだ。
== 近世以前 == === 山梨郡の立郡と管郷 === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|山梨岡神社]] [[画像:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|250px|thumb|寺本古代寺院跡]] 『[[和名類聚抄]]』国郡部（以下、『和名抄』）では甲斐四郡のうちはじめに記載され、「夜萬奈之」と訓じられている。於曾郷（おぞごう）、能呂郷（のろごう）、林部郷（はやしごう）、井上郷（いのえごう）、玉井郷（たまのいごう）、石禾郷（いさわごう）、表門郷（うわとごう）、山梨郷（やまなしごう）、加美郷（かみごう）、大野郷（おおのごう）の10の管郷を持ち、前者5郷が山梨東郡に、後者5郷が山梨西郡に含まれている。平安時代に成立した『和名抄』では管郷が二分されており、[[柏尾山経塚]]（甲州市、旧勝沼町）出土の康和5年（1103年）銘経筒にも山梨東郡の略称と考えられている「山東郡」の語が見られる。後期古墳時代の甲府盆地では東西に[[加牟那塚古墳]]を築造した盆地北西部勢力と[[姥塚古墳]]を築造した盆地東部の御坂勢力が対峙し、中間に[[春日居古墳群]]や[[寺本廃寺]]を築造した新興勢力が進出する状況が想定されているが、山梨郡では建郡当初から春日居勢力と御坂勢力が対峙する構造があったと考えられている。また、郡域には巨摩郡の[[飛び地]]も含まれている。
史料上の初見は『正倉院宝物』の調庸白あしぎぬ金青袋の墨書銘（和銅7年）や平城宮跡出土木簡の墨書銘（天平宝字6年、同8年）のほか、一宮町坪井の大原遺跡から出土した[[墨書土器]]や甲府市の[[大坪遺跡]]出土の[[刻書土器]]など、郷名が記された考古遺物も出土している。
=== 「山梨」の地名の由来と歴史 === 『和名抄』では「夜萬奈之」、「山無瀬」と訓じられている。和銅6年（[[713年]]）の「各地の地名は好字（縁起の良い漢字）二字で表せ」とする和銅官命の後から「山梨」の名前が定着し、使われ始めた。そのため、巷間に通説だとして流布している「山梨の由来は[[ヤマナシ]]（山梨）の木が多かったから」というものはいささか単純すぎるものであり、語源としては「'''山ならす（山平らす）'''」が「やまなし」へと転化してゆき、それに好字としての「梨」の字を当てたと見るのが有力である。「山ならす」とは、甲府盆地の東部に位置し、盆地を囲む山々を除けば高低の少ない平坦な土地であった当郡を表した言葉である。
『和名抄』では甲斐国府は八代郡に所在していたと記載され、『和名抄』の成立した後期国府は山梨・八代郡境にあたる御坂町国衙の地に想定されているが、山梨郡域にあたる旧春日居町域には「国府（こう）」や「鎮目」などの地名や県内最古の古代寺院である寺本廃寺や官衙遺跡または郡家遺跡と考えられている国府遺跡など『和名抄』成立以前の初期国府が所在していたと考えられている。また、一宮町域には[[甲斐国分寺]]・[[甲斐国分尼寺|国分尼寺]]跡が創建されているなど、山梨郡は古代甲斐国における政治的中心地であったと考えられている。
古代には在庁官人の三枝氏が勢力基盤とし、中世には甲斐守護[[武田氏]]の守護所が置かれた。戦国時代には[[甲府]]の地に（[[躑躅ヶ崎館]]）が築かれ、甲府は[[城下町]]として整備され近世に至るまで[[国中地方|国中地域]]や甲斐国の中心となった。
=== 式内社 === 『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]に記される郡内の[[式内社]]。 {{式内社一覧/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''山梨郡''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== 近世以降 == ''所属町村の変遷は[[東山梨郡#郡発足までの沿革]]、[[西山梨郡#郡発足までの沿革]]をそれぞれ参照'' * 「[[旧高旧領取調帳]]」に記載されている[[明治]]初年時点での支配は以下の通り。甲府総町は便宜的に1町に数える。（1町147村） ** 後の'''東山梨郡'''域（94村） - [[甲府代官所]]、石和代官所、[[田安徳川家]]領 ** 後の'''西山梨郡'''域（1町53村） - 甲府町奉行、甲府代官所、市川代官所、石和代官所 * [[慶応]]4年 ** 5月 - 甲府町奉行が廃止され、甲府総町は甲府町差配の管轄となる。 ** [[5月24日 (旧暦)|5月24日]]（[[1868年]][[7月13日]]） - 田安徳川家が立藩して'''[[田安徳川家|田安藩]]'''となる。 ** [[9月4日 (旧暦)|9月4日]]（1868年[[10月19日]]） - 甲府代官所・市川代官所・石和代官所の管轄地がそれぞれ'''[[府中県]]'''・'''[[市川県]]'''・'''[[石和県]]'''の管轄となる。 ** [[10月28日 (旧暦)|10月28日]]（1868年[[12月11日]]） - 田安藩領を除く全域が'''[[甲斐府]]'''の管轄となる。 * 明治2年 ** [[7月20日 (旧暦)|7月20日]]（[[1869年]][[8月27日]]） - 甲斐府が'''[[甲府県]]'''に改称。 ** [[12月26日 (旧暦)|12月26日]]（[[1870年]][[1月27日]]） - 田安藩が廃藩となり、翌年にかけて領地が甲府県の管轄となる。 * 明治3年[[11月20日 (旧暦)|11月20日]]（[[1871年]][[1月10日]]） - 甲府県が'''[[山梨県]]'''に改称。 * 明治4年（1871年）から明治8年（[[1875年]]）にかけて町村の再編が行われる。（1町46村） * 明治11年（[[1878年]]）[[12月19日]] - [[郡区町村編制法]]の山梨県での施行により、日下部村ほか31村の区域に'''[[東山梨郡]]'''、甲府総町ほか1町15村の区域に'''[[西山梨郡]]'''がそれぞれ行政区画として発足。同日山梨郡消滅。
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2013年12月}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|year=1984|date=1984-09-01|title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]]|publisher=[[角川書店]]|volume=19 山梨県|isbn=4040011902|ref={{SfnRef|角川日本地名大辞典|1984}}}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param 旧高旧領取調帳データベース] * [[山梨県道路公社]]『雁坂トンネルと秩父往還』[[1998年]] * [[平凡社]]『山梨県の地名』[[1995年]] * 原正人「郡郷の成立」『山梨県史通史編1原始・古代』
== 関連項目 == * [[国中地方]] * [[消滅した郡の一覧]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[山梨郡]] to [[Yamanashi District]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|令制国一覧|東山道|甲斐国}} * {{Pathnav|日本|中部地方|山梨県}} }} [[ファイル:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|山梨県山梨郡の範囲]] '''山梨郡'''（やまなしぐん）は、[[山梨県]]（[[甲斐国]]）にあった[[郡]]。
山梨県の県名はこの山梨郡に由来している。
== 郡域 == 消滅時の郡域は下記の区域にあたるが、行政区画として画定されたものではない。
* [[山梨市]]の全域 * [[甲州市]]の大部分（勝沼町上岩崎・勝沼町下岩崎・勝沼町藤井・大和町日影・大和町田野・大和町木賊を除く） * [[甲府市]]の一部（黒平町を除く概ね[[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]以東） * [[笛吹市]]の一部（春日居町小松・春日居町国府・春日居町鎮目・春日居町山崎・春日居町松本以北）
[[古代]]の郡域には、これらのほか、笛吹市一宮町各町・御坂町各町・石和町各町を含んだ。
== 近世以前 == === 山梨郡の立郡と管郷 === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|山梨岡神社]] [[画像:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|250px|thumb|寺本古代寺院跡]] 『[[和名類聚抄]]』国郡部（以下、『和名抄』）では甲斐四郡のうちはじめに記載され、「夜萬奈之」と訓じられている。於曾郷（おぞごう）、能呂郷（のろごう）、林部郷（はやしごう）、井上郷（いのえごう）、玉井郷（たまのいごう）、石禾郷（いさわごう）、表門郷（うわとごう）、山梨郷（やまなしごう）、加美郷（かみごう）、大野郷（おおのごう）の10の管郷を持ち、前者5郷が山梨東郡に、後者5郷が山梨西郡に含まれている。平安時代に成立した『和名抄』では管郷が二分されており、[[柏尾山経塚]]（甲州市、旧勝沼町）出土の康和5年（1103年）銘経筒にも山梨東郡の略称と考えられている「山東郡」の語が見られる。後期古墳時代の甲府盆地では東西に[[加牟那塚古墳]]を築造した盆地北西部勢力と[[姥塚古墳]]を築造した盆地東部の御坂勢力が対峙し、中間に[[春日居古墳群]]や[[寺本廃寺]]を築造した新興勢力が進出する状況が想定されているが、山梨郡では建郡当初から春日居勢力と御坂勢力が対峙する構造があったと考えられている。また、郡域には巨摩郡の[[飛び地]]も含まれている。
史料上の初見は『正倉院宝物』の調庸白あしぎぬ金青袋の墨書銘（和銅7年）や平城宮跡出土木簡の墨書銘（天平宝字6年、同8年）のほか、一宮町坪井の大原遺跡から出土した[[墨書土器]]や甲府市の[[大坪遺跡]]出土の[[刻書土器]]など、郷名が記された考古遺物も出土している。
=== 「山梨」の地名の由来と歴史 === 『和名抄』では「夜萬奈之」、「山無瀬」と訓じられている。和銅6年（[[713年]]）の「各地の地名は好字（縁起の良い漢字）二字で表せ」とする和銅官命の後から「山梨」の名前が定着し、使われ始めた。そのため、巷間に通説だとして流布している「山梨の由来は[[ヤマナシ]]（山梨）の木が多かったから」というものはいささか単純すぎるものであり、語源としては「'''山ならす（山平らす）'''」が「やまなし」へと転化してゆき、それに好字としての「梨」の字を当てたと見るのが有力である。「山ならす」とは、甲府盆地の東部に位置し、盆地を囲む山々を除けば高低の少ない平坦な土地であった当郡を表した言葉である。
『和名抄』では甲斐国府は八代郡に所在していたと記載され、『和名抄』の成立した後期国府は山梨・八代郡境にあたる御坂町国衙の地に想定されているが、山梨郡域にあたる旧春日居町域には「国府（こう）」や「鎮目」などの地名や県内最古の古代寺院である寺本廃寺や官衙遺跡または郡家遺跡と考えられている国府遺跡など『和名抄』成立以前の初期国府が所在していたと考えられている。また、一宮町域には[[甲斐国分寺]]・[[甲斐国分尼寺|国分尼寺]]跡が創建されているなど、山梨郡は古代甲斐国における政治的中心地であったと考えられている。
古代には在庁官人の三枝氏が勢力基盤とし、中世には甲斐守護[[武田氏]]の守護所が置かれた。戦国時代には[[甲府]]の地に（[[躑躅ヶ崎館]]）が築かれ、甲府は[[城下町]]として整備され近世に至るまで[[国中地方|国中地域]]や甲斐国の中心となった。
=== 式内社 === 『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]に記される郡内の[[式内社]]。 {{式内社一覧/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''山梨郡''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== 近世以降 == ''所属町村の変遷は[[東山梨郡#郡発足までの沿革]]、[[西山梨郡#郡発足までの沿革]]をそれぞれ参照'' * 「[[旧高旧領取調帳]]」に記載されている[[明治]]初年時点での支配は以下の通り。甲府総町は便宜的に1町に数える。（1町147村） ** 後の'''東山梨郡'''域（94村） - [[甲府代官所]]、石和代官所、[[田安徳川家]]領 ** 後の'''西山梨郡'''域（1町53村） - 甲府町奉行、甲府代官所、市川代官所、石和代官所 * [[慶応]]4年 ** 5月 - 甲府町奉行が廃止され、甲府総町は甲府町差配の管轄となる。 ** [[5月24日 (旧暦)|5月24日]]（[[1868年]][[7月13日]]） - 田安徳川家が立藩して'''[[田安徳川家|田安藩]]'''となる。 ** [[9月4日 (旧暦)|9月4日]]（1868年[[10月19日]]） - 甲府代官所・市川代官所・石和代官所の管轄地がそれぞれ'''[[府中県]]'''・'''[[市川県]]'''・'''[[石和県]]'''の管轄となる。 ** [[10月28日 (旧暦)|10月28日]]（1868年[[12月11日]]） - 田安藩領を除く全域が'''[[甲斐府]]'''の管轄となる。 * 明治2年 ** [[7月20日 (旧暦)|7月20日]]（[[1869年]][[8月27日]]） - 甲斐府が'''[[甲府県]]'''に改称。 ** [[12月26日 (旧暦)|12月26日]]（[[1870年]][[1月27日]]） - 田安藩が廃藩となり、翌年にかけて領地が甲府県の管轄となる。 * 明治3年[[11月20日 (旧暦)|11月20日]]（[[1871年]][[1月10日]]） - 甲府県が'''[[山梨県]]'''に改称。 * 明治4年（1871年）から明治8年（[[1875年]]）にかけて町村の再編が行われる。（1町46村） * 明治11年（[[1878年]]）[[12月19日]] - [[郡区町村編制法]]の山梨県での施行により、日下部村ほか31村の区域に'''[[東山梨郡]]'''、甲府総町ほか1町15村の区域に'''[[西山梨郡]]'''がそれぞれ行政区画として発足。同日山梨郡消滅。
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2013年12月}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会|year=1984|date=1984-09-01|title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]]|publisher=[[角川書店]]|volume=19 山梨県|isbn=4040011902|ref={{SfnRef|角川日本地名大辞典|1984}}}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param 旧高旧領取調帳データベース] * [[山梨県道路公社]]『雁坂トンネルと秩父往還』[[1998年]] * [[平凡社]]『山梨県の地名』[[1995年]] * 原正人「郡郷の成立」『山梨県史通史編1原始・古代』
== 関連項目 == * [[国中地方]] * [[消滅した郡の一覧]]

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|qq=}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|qq=}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|qq=}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|qq=}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years=Until [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Higashiyamanashi District]] / [[Nishiyamanashi District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古墳時代}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|qq=}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[district]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.

Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|qq=}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|qq=}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|qq=}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}

* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)

== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|qq=|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469930]] Wikidata name: Yamanashi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|nobars=yes}} {{s-ttl|title=Administrative Division Transition|years=Until [[1878}} {{s-aft|after=[[Higashiyamanashi District]] / [[Nishiyamanashi District]]|nobars=yes}} {{end}} {{Kai Province districts}}
{{translated page|ja|山梨郡|version=97228965|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 6]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469930]] Wikidata name: Yamanashi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.

=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469930]] Wikidata name: Yamanashi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469930]] Wikidata name: Yamanashi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469930]] Wikidata name: Yamanashi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]]).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * [[:en:List of dissolved districts in Japan|List of dissolved districts in Japan]]

== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the [[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyo Ruijusho]], Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of [[:en:Pyrus pyrifolia|yamanashi trees]] (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the [[:en:Kai Kokubun-ji|Kai Provincial Temple]]. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]], with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see [[:en:Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Higashiyamanashi District]] and [[:en:Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|Nishiyamanashi District]].''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
