revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469930]] Wikidata name: Yamanashi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a {{ill|District|en|District|WD=Q149621|lt=District}} in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (formerly {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|Pyrus pyrifolia|en|Pyrus pyrifolia|WD=Q838963|lt=yamanashi trees}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Kokubun-ji|en|Kai Kokubun-ji|WD=Q11577870|lt=Kai Provincial Temple}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q5754165|lt=Higashiyamanashi District}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q11627865|lt=Nishiyamanashi District}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|en|List of dissolved districts in Japan|WD=Q6616953|lt=List of dissolved districts in Japan}}

* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|Pyrus pyrifolia|en|Pyrus pyrifolia|WD=Q838963|lt=yamanashi trees}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Kokubun-ji|en|Kai Kokubun-ji|WD=Q11577870|lt=Kai Provincial Temple}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q5754165|lt=Higashiyamanashi District}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q11627865|lt=Nishiyamanashi District}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kunichu Region|ja|国中地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunichu Region|1=Kuninaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420406}} * {{ill|List of dissolved districts in Japan|en|List of dissolved districts in Japan|WD=Q6616953|lt=List of dissolved districts in Japan}}

The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.

Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|Pyrus pyrifolia|en|Pyrus pyrifolia|WD=Q838963|lt=yamanashi trees}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Kokubun-ji|en|Kai Kokubun-ji|WD=Q11577870|lt=Kai Provincial Temple}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q5754165|lt=Higashiyamanashi District}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q11627865|lt=Nishiyamanashi District}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q5754165|lt=Higashiyamanashi District|1=Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q11627865|lt=Nishiyamanashi District|1=Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Yamanashi-gun]][[ja:山梨郡]][[zh:山梨郡]]
{{Pathnavbox| * {{Pathnav|List of Ritsuryo Provinces|Tosando|Kai Province}} * {{Pathnav|Japan|Chubu Region|Yamanashi Prefecture}} }} [[File:Yamanashi Yamanashi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Yamanashi District in Yamanashi Prefecture]] '''Yamanashi District''' (山梨郡, やまなしぐん) was a {{ill|District|en|District|WD=Q149621|lt=District|1=District}} in {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} (formerly {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province|1=Kai Province}}).
The name of Yamanashi Prefecture originates from Yamanashi District.
== District Area == At the time of its dissolution, the district encompassed the following areas, though its boundaries were not administratively defined:
* All of [[Yamanashi City]] * Most of [[Koshu City]] (excluding Katsunuma Town Kami-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Shimo-Iwasaki, Katsunuma Town Fujii, Yamato Town Hikage, Yamato Town Tano, and Yamato Town Tokusa) * Part of [[Kofu City]] (generally east of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|qq=}}, excluding Kurobe Town) * Part of [[Fuefuki City]] (north of Kasugai Town Komatsu, Kasugai Town Kokufu, Kasugai Town Shizume, Kasugai Town Yamazaki, and Kasugai Town Matsumoto)
In ancient times, the district also included Ichinomiya Town, Misaka Town, and Isawa Town in Fuefuki City.
== Pre-modern History == === Formation and Administrative Divisions of Yamanashi District === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №1.jpg|thumb|250px|Yamanashi Oka Shrine]] [[File:Ruins of a temple of Teramoto in Fuefuki City Yamanashi Pref.jpg|thumb|250px|Ruins of Teramoto Ancient Temple]] In the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|WD=Q2702542|lt=Wamyo Ruijusho|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}, Yamanashi District was one of the four districts of Kai Province and was pronounced "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之). It had ten administrative subdistricts (kango), with the first five—Oso, Noro, Hayashibe, Inoe, and Tamanoi—belonging to Yamanashi East District, and the latter five—Isawa, Uwato, Yamanashi, Kami, and Ono—belonging to Yamanashi West District. In the {{ill|Late Kofun period|ja|古墳時代後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun period|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=古墳時代|1=Kofun period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q459939}}, the area was divided into competing factions, and the boundaries of the district may have reflected this rivalry.
Artifacts and inscriptions bearing the names of these subdistricts have been discovered at archaeological sites, such as {{ill|Onohara Site|ja|大原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onohara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ichinomiya Town and the {{ill|Otsubo Site|ja|大坪遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsubo Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kofu City.
=== Origins and Etymology of "Yamanashi" === The Wamyo Ruijusho spells the name as "Yamanashi" (夜萬奈之) or "Yamanase" (山無瀬). The district name became fixed as "Yamanashi" following an edict in Wado 6 (713) mandating the use of auspicious two-character kanji for place names. Contrary to popular belief that the name derives from an abundance of {{ill|Pyrus pyrifolia|en|Pyrus pyrifolia|WD=Q838963|lt=yamanashi trees|1=Pyrus pyrifolia}} (wild pears), it is more likely derived from "Yama narasu" (山ならす), meaning "to level mountains," symbolizing the flat terrain of the eastern Kofu Basin.
The district was a political and cultural center of ancient Kai Province, home to early government offices and Buddhist temples such as the {{ill|Kai Kokubun-ji|en|Kai Kokubun-ji|WD=Q11577870|lt=Kai Provincial Temple|1=Kai Kokubun-ji}}. In later centuries, it became a stronghold of the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}}, with their main castle in Kofu City.
=== Shrines Listed in the Engishiki === The district's shrines recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}: {{Shikinaisha list/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''Yamanashi District''' |- ||||||||||||||| |}
== Post-Meiji Restoration == ''For the history of municipalities within the district, see {{ill|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q5754165|lt=Higashiyamanashi District|1=Higashiyamanashi District, Yamanashi}} and {{ill|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|en|Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi|WD=Q11627865|lt=Nishiyamanashi District|1=Nishiyamanashi District, Yamanashi}}.''
* During the early Meiji period, the district's territories were distributed as follows (1 town, 147 villages): ** Former '''Higashiyamanashi District''' (94 villages) - under the control of Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and the {{ill|House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|ja|田安徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Tokugawa-Tayasu|1=Tayasu House|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576489}}. ** Former '''Nishiyamanashi District''' (1 town, 53 villages) - under the Kofu Magistrate's Office, Isawa Magistrate's Office, and Ichikawa Magistrate's Office. * Meiji 2 (1869) - Yamanashi Prefecture was established from the Kofu region. * Meiji 11 (1878) - The district was officially dissolved, with its areas reorganized into Higashiyamanashi and Nishiyamanashi Districts.
